stem: Let \( f(x) = [[m]]x + ([[b]]) \), and fix \( x_0 = [[x0]] \) and \( \varepsilon = \dfrac{[[eps_num]]}{[[eps_den]]} \). Using the \( \varepsilon\text{-}\delta \) definition of continuity, what is the LARGEST \( \delta > 0 \) such that \( |x - x_0| < \delta \) implies \( |f(x)-f(x_0)| < \varepsilon \)?
solution: Since \( |f(x)-f(x_0)| = |[[m]]||x-x_0| \), the condition \( |f(x)-f(x_0)|<\varepsilon \) holds exactly when \( |x-x_0| < \dfrac{\varepsilon}{|[[m]]|} \). So the largest valid \( \delta \) is \( \dfrac{\varepsilon}{|[[m]]|} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( |m| \) instead of dividing by it.
This ignores the slope entirely and just reports \( \varepsilon \).
This divides by \( m^{2} \) instead of \( |m| \).
This inverts the ratio of \( \varepsilon \) and \( |m| \).
This is off by one from the correct \( \delta \).
This is off by one from the correct \( \delta \).

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[m]]x + ([[b]]) \), and fix \( x_0 = [[x0]] \) and \( \delta = \dfrac{[[delta_num]]}{[[delta_den]]} \). What is the SMALLEST \( \varepsilon > 0 \) such that \( |x-x_0| < \delta \) is guaranteed to imply \( |f(x)-f(x_0)| < \varepsilon \) (i.e. the sharp bound on \( |f(x)-f(x_0)| \) over that \( \delta \)-neighborhood)?
solution: For \( |x-x_0|<\delta \), \( |f(x)-f(x_0)| = |[[m]]||x-x_0| \) ranges up to (but not including) \( |[[m]]|\delta \). So the sharp bound is \( \varepsilon = |[[m]]|\cdot\delta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( |m| \) instead of multiplying by it.
This ignores the slope entirely and just reports \( \delta \).
This multiplies by \( m^{2} \) instead of \( |m| \).
This inverts the ratio of \( |m| \) and \( \delta \).
This is off by one from the correct \( \varepsilon \).
This is off by one from the correct \( \varepsilon \).

stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{2} \) and fix \( x_0 = [[x0]] \), \( \varepsilon = \dfrac{[[eps_num]]}{[[eps_den]]} \). Using the standard bound (restricting attention to \( \delta \le 1 \), so that \( |x+x_0| \le 2x_0+1 \) whenever \( |x-x_0|<\delta \le 1 \)), the textbook choice \( \delta = \min\!\left(1, \dfrac{\varepsilon}{2x_0+1}\right) \) makes \( |x-x_0|<\delta \) imply \( |f(x)-f(x_0)|<\varepsilon \). Since \( \varepsilon \le 2x_0+1 \) here, what is this \( \delta \)?
solution: Since \( |f(x)-f(x_0)| = |x-x_0||x+x_0| \) and \( |x+x_0|\le 2x_0+1 \) once \( \delta\le 1 \), choosing \( \delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{2x_0+1} \) forces \( |f(x)-f(x_0)| < \delta(2x_0+1) = \varepsilon \). Because \( \varepsilon \le 2x_0+1 \) here, this \( \delta \) is already \( \le 1 \), so it is exactly \( \min(1,\varepsilon/(2x_0+1)) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the \( +1 \) from bounding \( |x+x_0| \) by \( 2x_0+1 \), using \( 2x_0 \) instead.
This multiplies by \( 2x_0+1 \) instead of dividing by it.
This always reports the cap \( \delta\le 1 \) rather than the tighter bound.
This is off by one from the correct \( \delta \).
This is off by one from the correct \( \delta \).
